raccoon-removal.metaTitle: Savannah Raccoon Removal | Licensed Chatham County Trappers
raccoon-removal.metaDescription: Professional raccoon removal in Savannah & Chatham County, GA. Historic District attics, Tybee Island, Pooler, Skidaway. Licensed, humane, same-day. Call now.
raccoon-removal.leadParagraph: Raccoons are the highest-volume year-round residential wildlife call in Chatham County, and the species behaves differently here than anywhere inland in Georgia. The combination of the Savannah Historic District's 1700s-1800s housing stock, the continuous live oak canopy under Spanish moss through Ardsley Park and Habersham Park, the salt marsh and tidal creek food supply across Wilmington Island, Whitemarsh Island, and Isle of Hope, the Tybee Island barrier-island habitat with its sea turtle nesting beaches, and the year-round mild coastal climate produces northern raccoons (Procyon lotor) that are larger, denser, and more food-conditioned than any inland Georgia population. Adult coastal raccoons in Chatham routinely run 18-30 pounds — well above the 10-15 pound state average — and they exploit residential entry points, attic spaces, chimneys, and pool decks across every neighborhood in the county.
raccoon-removal.extendedBody:
Why Chatham County Has the Highest Raccoon Density in Georgia

Four factors stack up in Chatham to produce raccoon densities that no inland Georgia county matches. First, the food supply runs year-round and across multiple food sources simultaneously. Tidal creeks across the eastside — Wilmington Island, Whitemarsh Island, Isle of Hope, Skidaway Island, and the salt marsh edges — feed Chatham raccoons fiddler crabs, marsh periwinkles, blue crabs, oysters, and stranded fish year-round. The Savannah Historic District tourism corridor — River Street, Bay Street, City Market, Broughton Street, and the Forsyth Park edge — adds restaurant-grade food density that sustains a permanent dumpster ecology. The barrier islands at Tybee, Wassaw, and Skidaway add seasonal sea turtle eggs (a major raccoon food source from May through August), and the suburban garbage and pet-food supply across Pooler, Garden City, Southside Savannah, and Bloomingdale closes the loop.

Second, the canopy. The Savannah Historic District live oaks are 200- to 300-year-old, evergreen, and run continuously through the city's iconic 22-square grid — providing arboreal travel routes between properties year-round, unlike the deciduous oak-hickory canopy in inland Georgia counties. Ardsley Park, Chatham Crescent, Habersham Park, Gordonston, and the older Eastside neighborhoods all sit under similarly continuous live oak canopy. A raccoon that can reach a Savannah live oak can reach a roofline within ten minutes.

Third, the climate. Coastal Georgia's mild winters allow Chatham raccoons to remain active and breed twelve months per year, with no deep cold to limit kit survival or trigger extended winter denning. This produces consistent population pressure across all four seasons that inland counties don't experience. Fourth, the housing stock. The Savannah Historic District's 1700s-1800s brick row houses, federal-style townhomes, and antebellum mansions — combined with the 1900s-1930s Ardsley Park and Chatham Crescent expansion — produce entry-point profiles unmatched in coastal Georgia. The result: adult Chatham raccoons routinely run 18-30 pounds, and population densities along the live oak canopy commonly exceed 25 raccoons per square mile — roughly twice the density of metro Atlanta neighborhoods.

Raccoons in Chatham County Neighborhoods

Raccoon activity isn't uniform across Chatham. The job mix on a Tuesday in the Savannah Historic District looks completely different from a Tuesday on Tybee Island, and that pattern has held steady for years.

Savannah Historic District (City Market, River Street, Forsyth Park, Madison Square)

The highest raccoon-pressure submarket in coastal Georgia. The 1700s-1800s housing stock — brick row houses, federal-style townhomes, antebellum mansions, and the iconic Savannah Square architecture — combines with River Street and Bay Street tourism food density and the continuous Historic District live oak canopy to produce attic and chimney infestation profiles unmatched in inland Georgia. Most exclusion jobs in this submarket find 5+ entry points, structural repair beyond standard sealing is routine, and any work has to coordinate with the Historic Savannah Foundation and city historic-preservation review for visible structural changes. Carriage-house attic spaces, third-story dormers, masonry chimneys with deteriorated mortar, and original wood gable vents are the typical entry profile.

Ardsley Park, Chatham Crescent, Habersham Park, and Gordonston

The 1900s-1930s historic-residential expansion just south of the Historic District — Ardsley Park, Chatham Crescent, Habersham Park, Gordonston, and the streets around Daffin Park. Continuous mature live oak canopy, Tudor and Colonial Revival housing with substantial wood architectural detail, and the Bonaventure Cemetery / Daffin Park / Forsyth Park corridor all combine to produce heavy raccoon pressure. Attic infestations are the dominant call type — gable vents, dormer junctions, soffit returns, and decayed fascia are the typical entry points, with 3-5 viable entry points per home being routine.

Eastside Savannah, Wilmington Island, Whitemarsh Island, Isle of Hope, Thunderbolt

Waterfront and tidal-creek-edge neighborhoods where the salt marsh sits directly against residential property lines. Salt marsh raccoon foraging behavior is fully on display in this submarket — raccoons forage at low tide on fiddler crabs, periwinkles, oysters, and stranded fish, then bring food remains onto decks, into outdoor kitchens, and around pool houses. Pool-house attic colonies, dock-area garbage cans, and pet-feeding stations near the water are the typical call drivers. Skunks, opossums, and the occasional otter round out the call mix.

Skidaway Island and The Landings

Gated, planned community with mature canopy and golf-course infrastructure. Raccoon pressure is steady year-round; the golf-course retention ponds and the wooded edges between fairways and homes are the main travel corridors. Attic infestations in the upscale housing stock typically involve fewer entry points than Historic District work but require careful exterior coordination with the property-association architectural review.

Tybee Island and the Barrier Islands

Beach community with vacation rental density, raised-foundation construction, and federally protected sea turtle nesting beaches. Tybee Island raccoon work has a federal regulatory layer that no other Chatham submarket has because raccoons are the documented number-one predator of sea turtle nests on the island, and any control work near the May-October nesting season has to coordinate with the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service and the Tybee Island Marine Science Center sea turtle program. Vacation-rental attic and crawl-space infestations spike in the off-season when properties sit unoccupied for weeks. Storm flooding from tropical systems regularly pushes raccoons into elevated structures across the island.

Southside Savannah, Sandfly, Habersham Woods, Windsor Forest

1970s-1990s suburban housing with mature canopy that has grown up around the homes in the decades since construction. Steady year-round raccoon pressure, typically simpler structurally than Historic District work. Entry points cluster around builder-grade ridge vents, roof-to-wall transitions, and decayed soffit returns.

Pooler, Bloomingdale, Garden City, Port Wentworth

Newer 2000s-2020s suburban construction in the western half of the county, plus the older industrial-edge neighborhoods of Garden City and Port Wentworth. Pooler in particular has seen rapid residential growth — newer subdivisions backing onto wooded edges and the I-95 / I-16 commercial corridor combine to produce a different raccoon-call profile than the historic intown work. Restaurant-district pressure along the Pooler Parkway and the airport corridor drives much of the call volume here.

Seasonal Patterns That Drive Chatham Raccoon Calls

Raccoon call volume in Chatham follows a coastal Georgia annual cycle that differs in important ways from inland Georgia. January through March is mating season — homeowners report fighting noises overhead, increased nighttime activity, and the first wave of attic intrusions as adult females scout den sites. Coastal mating activity runs hotter than inland because the mild winter never suppresses adult activity. March through May is birth season, when females settle into chimneys, attic cupolas, carriage-house lofts, and shed crawlspaces to whelp; emergency removal calls peak here, often involving 3-6 kits along with the mother. May through August is kit-rearing season AND sea turtle nesting season — the Tybee Island federal regulatory layer activates, and most exclusion work elsewhere in the county is timed around active kit dependency. September through November is dispersal AND Atlantic hurricane season, when juveniles strike out for new territory just as tropical systems push storm flooding into elevated structures across the eastside and barrier islands; this is the single highest call-volume window of the year for emergency raccoon removal in Chatham. December through February is the coastal Georgia equivalent of inland winter denning, but with greatly reduced activity suppression — Chatham raccoons often forgo extended winter denning entirely and continue active foraging through mild coastal winters.

Health and Safety Risks From Chatham County Raccoons

Georgia is a rabies-endemic state, and raccoons are the single most common rabies vector species in coastal Georgia. Any Chatham resident bitten or scratched by a raccoon should contact the Coastal Health District (Chatham County Health Department) immediately and not attempt to handle or release the animal. The Coastal Health District coordinates with the Georgia Department of Public Health on confirmed exposure cases. Beyond rabies, raccoon roundworm (Baylisascaris procyonis) is present in Chatham raccoon feces and is dangerous to humans and pets — particularly children who might come into contact with attic insulation contaminated by an infestation. Leptospirosis is a heightened concern in the coastal Georgia climate because the bacterium thrives in moist warm environments; raccoon urine in attic dust, contaminated standing water, and pool-deck travel paths all carry transmission risk. Canine distemper is fatal to unvaccinated dogs and can spread from raccoon contact. On the property side, raccoons in attics typically destroy 20-40% of the affected insulation, gnaw HVAC ductwork, and chew electrical wiring — a fire risk that homeowners' insurance underwriters in coastal Georgia take seriously, and one that's especially severe in the Historic District where original cloth-jacketed wiring may still be in service.

Sea Turtle Nest Predation: A Chatham-Specific Raccoon Issue

Chatham County has the only residential wildlife-control submarket in Georgia where raccoon work intersects directly with federal Endangered Species Act protections. Loggerhead, green, leatherback, and Kemp's ridley sea turtles nest on Tybee Island, Wassaw Island, and Ossabaw Island under federal ESA protection, and raccoons are documented as the single largest predator of sea turtle nests across the Georgia coast. The Tybee Island Marine Science Center, the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service, and the Georgia DNR Sea Turtle Cooperative coordinate annual nest protection that includes wire mesh nest screens specifically designed to deter raccoon predation. Beach-property owners on Tybee, Wassaw, and the surrounding islands may be subject to additional nest-protection requirements during the May-October nesting season, and any raccoon control work near nesting beaches has to be coordinated with the federal and state sea turtle program rather than executed independently. This federal regulatory layer is unique to Chatham among Georgia counties and is one reason coastal-Georgia raccoon work commands a higher operational threshold than inland counties.

Georgia Wildlife Regulations That Apply to Raccoon Removal in Chatham

Raccoons in Georgia are classified as both a game animal and a nuisance species, which puts them under a layered regulatory regime. Commercial removal in Chatham requires a Georgia DNR Wildlife Resources Division Trapping License, and any lethal control on a contract job requires a Nuisance Wildlife Control Permit. Chatham falls under Georgia DNR Wildlife Resources Division Coastal Region (Brunswick office), distinct from the Region 1 (Armuchee) office that covers metro Atlanta — coastal contractors register and coordinate through the Coastal Region rather than inland regions. Property owners may handle nuisance raccoons on their own property under specific conditions outlined in state regulations, but relocating live-trapped raccoons off-property is restricted, lethal control must comply with state hunting regulations, and any work in a sea turtle nesting area carries the additional federal ESA layer described above. Historic District structural work additionally requires coordination with the Historic Savannah Foundation and city preservation review for any visible structural changes. Every contractor operating in this directory holds the applicable state credentials and follows federal sea turtle protocol where required.

Our Chatham County Raccoon Removal Process

A typical Chatham raccoon removal job runs roughly as follows: an initial inspection of the attic, chimney, crawlspace, pool house, and the full exterior of the home; identification of every entry point (the average is 3-5 per infestation in suburban submarkets, 5+ in Historic District work, more on storm-damaged barrier-island properties); live trapping per Georgia DNR regulations or one-way exclusion doors when kits are present and active; professional sealing of all entry points using galvanized steel mesh, copper mesh, masonry repair, and code-appropriate flashing — coordinated with historic-preservation review where applicable; sanitation and decontamination of contaminated insulation, dropping zones, and travel paths using HEPA-equipped equipment; and damage repair, including insulation replacement and HVAC duct repair where needed. The full process from first call to final exclusion typically runs 5-14 days, longer when historic-preservation coordination is required or when sea turtle nesting season constrains the work window on barrier-island properties. See our full Chatham County wildlife removal coverage for the broader service area context including bats, snakes, opossums, and alligator referral.

faqs.q: How much does raccoon removal cost in Savannah and Chatham County?
faqs.a: Most full Chatham County raccoon jobs run between $500 and $1,500 from start to finish — somewhat higher than inland Georgia averages because of the multi-entry-point profiles typical in Savannah Historic District and Ardsley Park housing, the historic-preservation coordination required for visible structural changes, and the larger body size of coastal raccoons. The variables that move the price are the number of entry points that have to be sealed (Historic District homes routinely need 5+), whether kits are present (kit-season exclusion is more involved than adult removal), how much insulation and ductwork has to be replaced after sanitation, and whether the property requires Historic Savannah Foundation coordination or sea turtle nesting protocol on Tybee. Single-animal trap-and-release jobs at the low end run $300-$500; major Historic District attic remediations with full insulation replacement and structural sealing can exceed $2,500. Call for a free property-specific estimate.

faqs.q: How do I know if I have raccoons in my Savannah Historic District home?
faqs.a: The first sign is almost always sound: heavy thumping, scratching, or chittering from the attic, especially around dusk and just before dawn. Coastal Chatham raccoons are larger than inland raccoons (often 18-30 lbs), and homeowners commonly describe the sound as 'someone walking up there.' Other signs include damaged fascia or soffits, claw marks on downspouts and gutters, droppings on the roof or in courtyards, and the smell of urine penetrating ceiling drywall or upper-story closet ceilings. In Historic District homes specifically, look for disturbed plaster in third-story rooms, droppings around carriage-house entrances, and scratching at chimney flues and fireplace dampers — a Historic District chimney is a classic raccoon den site, particularly in spring and winter.

faqs.q: Are raccoons in Chatham County dangerous to my family or pets?
faqs.a: Yes, in specific ways. Coastal Georgia is rabies-endemic and raccoons are the most common rabies vector — any bite or scratch should be reported to the Coastal Health District (Chatham County Health Department) immediately. Raccoon roundworm (Baylisascaris procyonis) is present in their feces and is genuinely dangerous to children and pets that come into contact with contaminated insulation. Leptospirosis is a heightened risk in coastal Georgia's warm, moist climate — the bacterium thrives in standing water and pool-deck travel paths and can be transmitted through dried urine in attic dust. Canine distemper from raccoon contact is fatal to unvaccinated dogs. Healthy raccoons generally avoid people, but a raccoon active in daylight, behaving disoriented, or aggressive should be treated as potentially rabid and reported.

faqs.q: Why are Chatham County raccoons so much larger than inland Georgia raccoons?
faqs.a: Three things compound. First, the year-round mild coastal climate means Chatham raccoons never go through the food-scarcity pressure that limits inland populations during cold months. Second, the food supply is exceptional: tidal-creek seafood (fiddler crabs, periwinkles, oysters, fish), Savannah Historic District tourism-corridor food density, sea turtle eggs from May-August on the barrier islands, and continuous suburban garbage and pet-food access. Third, the breeding cycle: with no winter activity suppression, females reproduce more reliably and kits survive at higher rates. The result is a coastal raccoon population where adult body weights routinely run 18-30 pounds — well above the 10-15 pound state average — and population densities along the Historic District live oak canopy commonly exceed 25 raccoons per square mile, roughly twice the density of metro Atlanta neighborhoods.

faqs.q: Can I trap raccoons myself in Chatham County, Georgia?
faqs.a: Property owners can take some action against nuisance raccoons on their own property under Georgia regulations, but the rules are restrictive and the practical risks are high. Relocating a live-trapped raccoon off your property is regulated, lethal control must comply with state hunting regulations, and any handling carries a real rabies-exposure risk in a rabies-endemic state. Commercial trapping in Georgia requires a Georgia DNR Trapping License (issued through the Coastal Region office for Chatham), and Nuisance Wildlife Control Permits are required for paid lethal control. Any work near sea turtle nesting beaches on Tybee, Wassaw, or the other barrier islands during the May-October nesting season has to coordinate with the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service and the Georgia DNR Sea Turtle Cooperative. For most Chatham homeowners, hiring a licensed coastal Georgia operator is faster, safer, and legally cleaner.

faqs.q: Do raccoons threaten sea turtle nests on Tybee Island?
faqs.a: Yes — raccoons are documented as the single largest predator of sea turtle nests across the Georgia coast, and the issue is particularly acute on Tybee Island. Loggerhead, green, leatherback, and Kemp's ridley sea turtles nest on Tybee, Wassaw, and Ossabaw under federal Endangered Species Act protection. The Tybee Island Marine Science Center, the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service, and the Georgia DNR Sea Turtle Cooperative coordinate annual nest protection that includes wire mesh nest screens specifically designed to deter raccoon predation. Beach-property owners on Tybee may be subject to additional nest-protection requirements during the May-October nesting season, and raccoon control work in nesting areas has to be coordinated with the federal and state sea turtle program rather than executed independently.

faqs.q: How long does professional raccoon removal take in Chatham County?
faqs.a: From first call to fully sealed and remediated home, expect 5 to 14 days for routine work — longer when historic-preservation coordination is required (Savannah Historic District, Ardsley Park, designated historic properties) or when sea turtle nesting season constrains the work window on barrier-island properties. The first 1-3 days are inspection and trap setting (or one-way exclusion door installation when kits are present). The next 3-7 days handle the actual removal — this is longer when a mother and kits are involved because exclusion has to wait until the kits are mobile. Final sealing, sanitation, and any insulation or ductwork repair adds another 1-3 days. Historic District homes with multiple entry points and architectural review requirements run on the longer end of that range, sometimes 21-30 days when full preservation coordination is required.

faqs.q: Do you handle raccoon removal in Pooler, Tybee Island, and the rest of Chatham County?
faqs.a: Yes — coverage includes the full Chatham County footprint: Savannah (Historic District, Ardsley Park, Chatham Crescent, Habersham Park, Gordonston, the Eastside, Southside, Sandfly, Habersham Woods, Windsor Forest), Pooler, Garden City, Port Wentworth, Bloomingdale, Tybee Island, Thunderbolt, and the unincorporated areas across Wilmington Island, Whitemarsh Island, Skidaway Island, Isle of Hope, and the Vernonburg / Beaulieu / Coffee Bluff stretches. Same-day response is available across the county. The contractor handling Chatham is licensed under Georgia DNR Coastal Region (Brunswick office), holds the applicable Trapping License and Nuisance Wildlife Control Permit, and follows federal sea turtle nest protection protocol where required on barrier-island work.
